summary: Gruber pushes back on Gene Munster's 5-year Apple forecast, particularly the predictions about dedicated AR glasses and AirPods becoming an augmented audio platform. He argues that the iPhone is the ideal form factor for mainstream augmented reality — great camera, great display, already in everyone's pocket — and that dedicated AR glasses only make sense for specialized industrial use. He contends ARKit isn't laying groundwork for a future AR device but is itself Apple's move into augmented reality, useful on iPhones and iPads today. On AirPods, Gruber sees them as great wireless headphones rather than a future AR audio platform, and predicts their price will drop rather than rise.
key_points: Dedicated AR glasses don't make sense for mainstream daily use; the phone's camera, display, and ubiquity make it the ideal casual AR form factor
ARKit is not a foundation for future AR hardware — it is Apple's augmented reality play, useful on iPhones and iPads right now
Apple doesn't add frameworks to iOS to 'set the foundation' for future products; there was no Mac OS X precursor to iPhone touchscreen apps
AirPods are wireless headphones, not an augmented audio platform, and their price will likely drop rather than rise over time
If Apple does make AR glasses, they would be an iPhone peripheral like Apple Watch, not a phone replacement
money_quotes: I'm hard pressed to think of anything we do today on our phones that would be better using AR glasses. Anything.
ARKit is not laying the foundation for Apple's future move into augmented reality. ARKit is Apple's move into augmented reality.

People don't want to wear glasses all the time, and even if they do, augmented reality is useful and interesting for an entirely different set of things than what a touchscreen interface is useful for.
Consider: there was nothing added to Mac OS X a decade ago to 'set the foundation' for writing touchscreen-based apps for the iPhone.
AirPods are just wireless headphones. The AR device is the iPhone, and AirPods are just a great audio output device for the iPhone.
I think the average selling price for AirPods will drop, not rise, over time. If Apple could sell them profitably for $99 today, I think they would.
key_insight: The phone is already the perfect mainstream AR device — great camera, great display, always with you — and Apple's ARKit exists because AR is useful on iPhones today, not as a stepping stone to dedicated glasses.
